does: This paper introduces MedBench v4, a comprehensive benchmarking infrastructure for evaluating Chinese medical AI systems across multiple modalities and agent architectures. The benchmark comprises over 700,000 expert-curated tasks spanning 24 primary and 91 secondary medical specialties, with rigorous multi-stage refinement and multi-round clinical review from over 500 institutions. The authors evaluate 15 frontier models across three tracks: large language models, multimodal models, and intelligent agents. Key findings reveal that base LLMs achieve a mean score of 54.1/100 with safety performance critically low at 18.4/100, multimodal models underperform at 47.5/100 despite reasonable perception capabilities, and agent-based systems substantially improve performance to 79.8/100 on average, with Claude Sonnet 4.5-based agents reaching 85.3/100 overall and 88.9/100 on safety-critical tasks.
connects: This work extends prior medical benchmarking efforts such as MedQA, PubMedQA, and earlier versions of MedBench by incorporating substantially larger task volumes, Chinese medical context, multimodal evaluation, and agent-based assessment. It responds to growing recognition that static question-answer benchmarks inadequately capture clinical workflows and safety requirements in medical AI deployment. The paper builds on emerging practices of using LLM-as-a-judge scoring methodologies calibrated to human ratings, while addressing documented gaps in safety and ethics evaluation of medical models. It connects to literature on agentic AI systems and their ability to improve performance through tool use and reasoning, as well as multimodal learning research showing current challenges in cross-modal reasoning within medical domains.
enables: This benchmark infrastructure enables systematic evaluation and improvement of medical AI systems specifically adapted to Chinese clinical contexts and practices. The identification of critical safety and ethics deficiencies in current models points toward urgent directions for alignment and robustness research in medical LLMs. The substantial performance improvements demonstrated by agent-based approaches suggest future work exploring how tool integration, retrieval augmentation, and multi-step reasoning can be leveraged for clinical deployment. The multimodal evaluation framework opens investigation into improving cross-modal reasoning in medical contexts, moving beyond perception-only strengths. The infrastructure itself, deployed as a cloud-based nationwide platform, enables ongoing evaluation of successive model releases and supports development of medical AI systems meeting higher safety and clinical utility standards for real-world deployment.
